expectations.text: Output contains an explicit ASVS level decision section with rationale
expectations.evidence: Section 1 is titled 'ASVS Level Decision' (line 13) and contains an explicit 'Rationale' subsection (line 17) with bulleted rationale items including 'Why Level 1 is insufficient', 'Why Level 2 is the right baseline', 'Why selective Level 3', and 'Internal/SSO context' (lines 19-24). An 'Out-of-Scope ASVS Areas (with rationale)' subsection (line 26) further explains exclusions.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 2 (or escalates specific features to 2/3) — Level 1 alone is insufficient for personnel data
expectations.evidence: Line 15 explicitly states: 'Selected Baseline: ASVS Level 2 (with selective Level 3 controls on the bulk reassignment and CSV export flows).' The rationale explicitly explains why Level 1 is insufficient (line 19) and which two flows escalate to L3 (F-03 and F-04, lines 22-23).

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Feature-ASVS Coverage Matrix' (line 34) contains a properly formatted markdown table (lines 36-66) with columns Feature, ASVS Section/Req, Level, Coverage, PRD Change Needed. Rows cover F-01, F-02, F-03, F-04, and ALL (cross-cutting) entries with explicit ASVS section IDs and coverage status.

expectations.text: Output covers all four features F-01, F-02, F-03, and F-04 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: All four features F-01 (line 74), F-02 (line 104), F-03 (line 135), F-04 (line 167) are present in Section 3 'Enhanced Feature Specifications'. However, the per-feature shape does NOT match the prescribed shape — feature blocks contain Description, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Updated Acceptance Criteria, and Securability Notes, but lack explicit 'Actor', 'Data', and 'Trust Boundaries' sections required by the per-feature shape per expectation #5. Marked failed because the prescribed shape is incomplete.

expectations.text: Each feature block includes Actor, Data, Trust Boundaries, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, and Securability Notes
expectations.evidence: Each feature block (F-01 lines 74-100, F-02 lines 104-131, F-03 lines 135-163, F-04 lines 167-198) includes 'Description', 'ASVS Mapping', 'Updated Requirements', 'Updated Acceptance Criteria', and 'Securability Notes' — but none of the four feature blocks have explicit 'Actor', 'Data', or 'Trust Boundaries' subsections. Some actor/data/boundary information is woven implicitly into Description and Securability Notes (e.g., F-01 line 100 mentions 'trust-boundary discipline (S2.6)'), but no feature block contains the three required labeled sections. Strict reading: failed.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational ('must be secure')
expectations.evidence: Acceptance criteria across all four features are concrete and observable. Examples: F-01 line 93 'receives 403 and produces a logged authorization-failure event'; line 96 'No URL or browser history entry contains a raw employee ID'; line 97 'Network capture shows no plaintext HTTP'; line 98 'appears in the centralized log store within 60 seconds'. F-03 line 158 'A cell starting with `=` in source data appears in the CSV with the leading character neutralized'. F-04 line 192 'audit log... can be reconstructed end-to-end by operation_id and shows: actor, source, target, every affected review, timestamps, and outcome.' These specify exact test observables (HTTP status codes, log fields, latency bounds, content shape) rather than aspirational language.

expectations.text: F-03 (CSV export) flags audit logging of export events and access control on the export endpoint
expectations.evidence: F-03 R-03.1 (line 144) requires a dedicated 'hr.review.export' entitlement, deny-by-default — access control on the endpoint. R-03.2 (line 145) enforces server-constrained region scoping. R-03.7 (line 150) requires logging '...every export emits a structured event including who, when, region, row_count, fields_included, compensation_included (boolean), justification, and a correlation id.' Acceptance criterion line 156 also tests authorization with a logged event.

expectations.text: F-04 (bulk reassign) flags authorization checks for the privileged operation and audit logging of the action
expectations.evidence: F-04 R-04.1 (line 176) requires a dedicated 'hr.review.reassign.admin' role with deny-by-default. R-04.2 (line 177) requires step-up authentication. R-04.9 (line 184) explicitly requires audit events: 'reassign.bulk.start', per-review 'reassign.review', and 'reassign.bulk.complete' with operation_id, actor, source/target manager, review_id, UTC timestamp, and outcome. R-04.10 (line 185) requires append-only log forwarding.

expectations.text: F-02 (private HR notes) addresses access scoping so notes are not exposed to the employee or manager
expectations.evidence: F-02 R-02.1 (line 113) explicitly states: 'Only users with hr.commentary.write may create or edit notes; only users with hr.commentary.read may read them. Employees and managers MUST NOT receive this entitlement.' R-02.2 (line 114) adds that 'Commentary notes are excluded from any read endpoint accessible to non-HR roles, including any future self-service surface. Backend filtering (not UI hiding) enforces this (V8.3.1).' Acceptance line 125 tests response shape parity to prevent field-presence side channels.

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' (line 202) is a dedicated section explicitly stating 'These apply across all features and should be tracked once, not duplicated per feature.' It contains 8 subsections: 4.1 Identity/Session/Trust Boundary, 4.2 Authorization, 4.3 Data Classification and Protection, 4.4 Transport and Configuration, 4.5 Input Validation and Output Encoding, 4.6 Logging and Audit, 4.7 Error Handling and Resilience, 4.8 Cross-Cutting Securability Notes. Requirements are labeled X-IDP-*, X-AZ-*, X-DC-*, X-TR-*, X-CFG-*, X-IV-*, X-LOG-*, X-ER-*.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section 7 'Open Gaps and Assumptions' (line 289) contains three subsections: 'Assumptions' (line 291) with A1-A4, 'Open Gaps Requiring Stakeholder Input' (line 298) with G1-G10 (retention windows, bulk-action upper bound, export caps, cross-region policy, justification content, undo window, privacy regulation scope, peer feedback attribution, threat-model review, logging budget), and 'Items Explicitly Marked Not Applicable' (line 311) with NA1-NA4.

expectations.text: Output contains an explicit ASVS level decision section with rationale
expectations.evidence: A dedicated '## ASVS Level Decision' section is present at lines 13-22, with 'Chosen Level: 2' followed by a multi-sentence Rationale explaining why L1 is insufficient and L3 over-scoped, plus a 'Feature-Level Escalations' subsection naming F-03 and F-04.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 2 (or escalates specific features to 2/3) — Level 1 alone is insufficient for personnel data
expectations.evidence: 'Chosen Level: 2' is stated explicitly. Rationale says 'Level 1 is insufficient because it does not require field-level authorization, audit logging of authorization decisions, sensitive-data classification, or rate/limit controls.' F-03 and F-04 are escalated to specific L3 controls (V14.2.6, V16.3.2, V2.3.5, V8.4.2).

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section '## Feature ↔ ASVS Coverage Matrix' contains a markdown table with columns 'Feature | ASVS Section | Requirement ID | Level | Coverage | PRD Change Needed' and ~35 populated rows covering F-01 through F-04 and cross-cutting items, with explicit Coverage values (Missing/Partial).

expectations.text: Output covers all four features F-01, F-02, F-03, and F-04 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Under '## Enhanced Feature Specifications' there are four sub-sections: 'Feature F-01: View Employee Performance Review', 'Feature F-02: Add HR Commentary', 'Feature F-03: Export Region Report (CSV)', and 'Feature F-04: Bulk Reassign Reviewer'. Each follows the same shape (Actor, Data, Trust Boundaries, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Securability Notes).

expectations.text: Each feature block includes Actor, Data, Trust Boundaries, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, and Securability Notes
expectations.evidence: All four feature sections (F-01 lines 71-103, F-02 lines 107-134, F-03 lines 138-167, F-04 lines 171-202) include all seven labeled subsections. Spot check F-04: 'Actor: HR Admin...', 'Data: Set of pending review records...', 'Trust Boundaries: browser → application server...', 'ASVS Mapping: V2.2.1, V2.2.3...', 'Updated Requirements:', 'Acceptance Criteria:', 'Securability Notes:'.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational ('must be secure')
expectations.evidence: Acceptance criteria specify concrete HTTP codes, log entry names, and observable behaviors. Examples: 'returns HTTP 403, no review body, and writes an authz.deny log entry with caller ID, target employee ID, region, and decision reason'; 'The 6th export in a 24-hour rolling window per user returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header'; 'A simulated mid-commit datastore failure leaves zero affected reviews mutated; a follow-up read of any of them returns the original assignedManager'.

expectations.text: F-03 (CSV export) flags audit logging of export events and access control on the export endpoint
expectations.evidence: F-03 Updated Requirements: 'The export feature requires a separate "HR Reporting" entitlement, distinct from review-read (V8.2.1)' and 'Every export request is logged with: requester, region scope, row count, columns included, request timestamp, completion timestamp, and outcome (success / denied / failed).' Also includes 'An export request whose region scope evaluates to zero rows still produces an audit log entry'.

expectations.text: F-04 (bulk reassign) flags authorization checks for the privileged operation and audit logging of the action
expectations.evidence: F-04 Updated Requirements: 'The bulk-reassign capability is restricted to the HR Admin role, enforced server-side; UI gating is presentation only (V8.2.1)' and 'Every step (propose, approve, reject, commit, fail) is logged with actor, action, source manager, target manager, and the resulting affected review-ID set'. Also adds step-up auth (V8.4.2 escalated) and four-eyes approval (V2.3.5).

expectations.text: F-02 (private HR notes) addresses access scoping so notes are not exposed to the employee or manager
expectations.evidence: F-02 Updated Requirements: '"Private to HR" is a server-side authorization rule, not a UI hide: any read API used by employees or managers must structurally exclude commentary fields, and a separate read endpoint exists for HR commentary (V8.2.3).' Acceptance Criteria reinforces with a contract test: 'An employee or manager calling any review-read endpoint never sees commentary in the response body, verified by a contract test.'

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' (lines 206-223) lists 13 cross-cutting controls including centralized authorization policy, centralized structured audit logging, tamper-resistant audit pipeline, output encoding, input validation, TLS baseline, session/SSO handling, classification, anti-caching, service-account least privilege, generic error handling, configuration hygiene, and dependency policy.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Open Gaps and Assumptions' (lines 227-242) enumerates 12 specific items requiring resolution, including regulatory scope, role model definition, data-classification table, SSO/IdP details, audit log retention, CSV temp-file retention, peer-feedback authorship, employee subject-access pathway, four-eyes feasibility, compensation-note presence by region, threat model, and SLA interaction with rate limits.

expectations.text: Output contains an explicit ASVS level decision section with rationale
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'ASVS Level Decision' (lines 11-24) opens with 'Selected baseline: ASVS Level 2.' followed by an itemized rationale that explicitly explains why not L1 (insufficient for account recovery; no MFA, no breached-password checks, no lifetime-limited recovery tokens) and why not L3 (no in-system payments, no regulated data, general consumer base). Targeted L3 escalations (V6.3.7, V6.3.8) are also justified.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 2 (with optional escalations) - appropriate for B2C SaaS holding PII
expectations.evidence: Line 13: 'Selected baseline: ASVS Level 2.' Rationale (line 16) explicitly cites '~250k MAU' and 'PII for ~250k MAU - exactly the profile ASVS calls out for L2'. Targeted L3 escalations applied for V6.3.7 (notify on credential reset) and V6.3.8 (no enumeration), which is a reasonable B2C posture.

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Feature-ASVS Coverage Matrix' (lines 27-74) is a markdown table with columns: Feature | ASVS § | Req ID | Lvl | Coverage | PRD Change Needed. Includes ~40 rows covering F-01, F-02, F-03, F-04, and 'All' (cross-cutting). Coverage status legend (C/P/M/N/A) is defined.

expectations.text: Output covers all four features F-01, F-02, F-03, and F-04 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Section 3 covers each of F-01 (lines 80-117), F-02 (lines 121-148), F-03 (lines 152-181), F-04 (lines 185-213). Each feature follows a consistent shape: User story (unchanged), ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements (numbered), Acceptance Criteria (testable, IDed), and Securability Notes (mapping to SSEM attributes).

expectations.text: F-01 (forgot password) requires identical response for valid and invalid emails to prevent account enumeration
expectations.evidence: F-01 Requirement 4 (line 91) titled 'Uniform response (no enumeration)' mandates HTTP 200 with generic message for any submitted email (registered, unregistered, malformed, locked) within bounded time envelope, with response-time variance <=50 ms enforced via async email send after constant-time response. AC-01.3 (line 110) verifies byte-identical response body and 95th-percentile timing delta <=50 ms across 100 trials.

expectations.text: F-01 reset tokens are specified as short-lived (minutes), single-use, and stored as a hash (or equivalent control)
expectations.evidence: F-01 Req 1 (line 88): CSPRNG, >=128 bits entropy, single-use, server-side bound. Req 2 (line 89): expire <=10 minutes. Req 3 (line 90): only hash persisted server-side (SHA-256 or stronger); plaintext exists only in email/URL. AC-01.1 verifies expiry; AC-01.2 verifies single-use with reuse-attempt logging.

expectations.text: F-01 and F-02 flows include rate limiting per email and/or per source IP
expectations.evidence: F-01 Req 5 (lines 92-95): three-layer rate limiting - per email (<=3/15min), per IP (<=10/15min), and global circuit-break. F-02 Req 5 (line 133): unlock issuance follows the same per-email and per-IP envelope as F-01. AC-01.4 and AC-02.6 verify enforcement.

expectations.text: F-03 recovery codes are required to be hashed at rest and invalidated on use
expectations.evidence: F-03 Req 2 (line 161): 'Codes MUST be hashed with an approved password-hashing algorithm (Argon2id preferred; bcrypt cost >=12 acceptable) using a per-code 32-bit (or larger) random salt. Plaintext codes are presented to the user once and never persisted plaintext server-side.' Req 4 (line 163): atomic single-use enforcement under transaction or compare-and-set. AC-03.2 verifies atomicity with 50 parallel submissions yielding exactly one success; AC-03.4 verifies no plaintext in any store.

expectations.text: F-04 support-agent bypass requires audit logging and identity verification controls (or dual control)
expectations.evidence: F-04 Req 1 (line 193): version-controlled identity verification script. Req 4 (line 196): two-channel confirmation requiring a second/supervisor agent (different identity) for combined password+MFA reset. Req 6 (line 198): audit trail with timestamp, agent_id, supervisor_id, ticket_id, action_type, target_user_id, source IP, outcome; >=1 year retention in immutable store. AC-04.4 verifies dual-agent commit; AC-04.5 verifies log emission to SIEM within 5 minutes.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational
expectations.evidence: ACs across all features cite specific, verifiable observables: 'T+10min+1s returns generic invalid-token response' (AC-01.1), 'p95 timing delta <=50 ms' (AC-01.3), 'chi-squared randomness check at alpha=0.01' (AC-03.1), '50 parallel submissions yields exactly one success' (AC-03.2), 'database dump inspection reveals no plaintext codes' (AC-03.4), '403 response and authz log event' (AC-04.1), 'two distinct agent identities in audit log before commit' (AC-04.4), 'logs in SIEM within 5 minutes' (AC-04.5). Aspirational language ('must be secure') is absent.

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' (lines 217-268) with 8 subsections: 4.1 Anti-Automation & Rate Limiting, 4.2 Authentication-Pathway Consistency, 4.3 Transport & Cookie Security, 4.4 Data Classification & Privacy, 4.5 Security Logging, 4.6 Cryptographic Hygiene, 4.7 Email Delivery Integrity, 4.8 Latency Budget. Each cites specific ASVS sections and includes acceptance criteria where applicable (AC-X.1, AC-X.2).

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section 5 'Open Gaps and Assumptions' (lines 272-299) lists 6 explicit Assumptions (A1-A6: existing logging/SIEM, KMS, SendGrid SPF/DKIM/DMARC config, classification register, support tooling roles, TOTP-based 2FA) and 7 Open Gaps (G1-G7: SMS channel, multi-user approval threshold, lockout-counter scope, enumeration vs UX trade-off, completely-lost-credentials path, adaptive risk signals, bot detection layer). Also revises original Acceptance section with three added measurable items.

expectations.text: Output contains an explicit ASVS level decision section with rationale
expectations.evidence: Section '## ASVS Level Decision' explicitly states 'Chosen Level: 2' followed by a multi-sentence rationale tying the choice to ~250k MAU, PII, GDPR, and account-takeover impact, plus a 'Feature-Level Escalations' subsection that explicitly escalates F-04 to L3-equivalent. The rationale also explains why L1 is insufficient and L3 is not justified, which is stronger than a bare assertion.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 2 (with optional escalations) — appropriate for B2C SaaS holding PII
expectations.evidence: Level 2 is chosen as the baseline, with a documented per-feature escalation: 'F-04 (Support-agent recovery): Treat as L3-equivalent for identity-proofing and audit.' The F-04 ASVS mapping reflects this with notations like '6.4.4 (treated as 6.4.6 for L3)' and 'Level 2 → 3'.

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section '## Feature ↔ ASVS Coverage Matrix' contains a proper markdown table with columns: Feature, ASVS Section, Requirement ID, Level, Coverage, PRD Change Needed. The table covers all four features and 24 distinct rows mapping ASVS sections (V2.2, V6.x, V7.4, V8.x, V11.4, V12.1, V14.3, V16.x) to specific PRD-change actions.

expectations.text: Output covers all four features F-01, F-02, F-03, and F-04 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Each of F-01, F-02, F-03, F-04 has a consistent structure: Actor, Data, Trust Boundaries, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements, Acceptance Criteria, Securability Notes. The same headings appear under each feature, and each section is non-trivially populated.

expectations.text: F-01 (forgot password) requires identical response for valid and invalid emails to prevent account enumeration
expectations.evidence: F-01 Updated Requirements: 'The system always returns the same generic success response — same body, same status code, same approximate timing — whether or not the email matches an account, to prevent enumeration (V6.3.8).' Reinforced by an acceptance criterion: 'Submitting a reset request for nobody@example.com (no account) returns the same response body, HTTP status, and response time (within ±50 ms of the median for valid accounts) as a request for an existing account.'

expectations.text: F-01 reset tokens are specified as short-lived (minutes), single-use, and stored as a hash (or equivalent control)
expectations.evidence: F-01: 'Reset tokens are generated using a CSPRNG with at least 128 bits of entropy, are single-use, and expire 15 minutes after issuance. Tokens are stored only as a salted hash; the raw token never persists server-side after delivery (V6.4.1).' Acceptance criteria include rejection at >15 minutes and replay rejection.

expectations.text: F-01 and F-02 flows include rate limiting per email and/or per source IP
expectations.evidence: F-01: 'Reset requests are anti-automated: rate-limited per email address, per source IP, and per CAPTCHA-challenged threshold.' AC: 'More than 5 reset requests for the same email in any 10-minute window result in HTTP 429.' F-02: 'unlock-email requests are rate-limited per account and per source IP.' AC: 'More than 5 unlock-email requests for the same account within 10 minutes returns 429.'

expectations.text: F-03 recovery codes are required to be hashed at rest and invalidated on use
expectations.evidence: F-03: 'Codes are stored at rest only as outputs of an approved password-hashing KDF (Argon2id preferred, bcrypt acceptable) with a per-code random salt' and 'Each code is single-use; on successful redemption the code is marked consumed and cannot be used again.' AC: 'A unit/integration test confirms that retrieving a stored recovery code from the database returns a hash, not plaintext, and that two identical-input generations produce different stored values (proving per-code salt).' AC: 'A recovery code redeemed once is rejected on a second redemption.'

expectations.text: F-04 support-agent bypass requires audit logging and identity verification controls (or dual control)
expectations.evidence: Audit logging: 'Each bypass produces an immutable audit record containing: agent ID, agent session ID, target user ID, UTC timestamp, proofing signals attested, reason category, free-text reason, source IP, and outcome. The audit record is shipped to a logically separate, append-only log store.' Identity verification: 'Identity proofing must be performed at strength equivalent to original enrollment. The verbal challenge alone is insufficient: the agent must collect at least two independent proofing signals from a documented list... Knowledge-based questions like favorite pet are explicitly forbidden.' Plus periodic second-function review of the bypass log (a soft dual-control). RBAC permission 'account.recovery.bypass' is also required.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational
expectations.evidence: Acceptance criteria across all four features cite specific HTTP statuses (401/403/429), numeric timing tolerances (±50 ms), specific log event names (reset.token.expired, reset.token.replay, reset.ratelimit.email, recovery_codes.lowwatermark), specific test mechanics (DevTools inspection of localStorage/sessionStorage/IndexedDB/cookies; integration test injecting an alt_email field; destructive-action test against a non-prod copy; diffing audit pipeline against expectations). No 'must be secure' or 'should be robust' style language was found.

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' enumerates 11 cross-cutting controls: TLS-everywhere, centralized recovery-token module, centralized password-hashing module, structured security logging, anti-automation baseline, generic error responses, SendGrid email channel hygiene, session termination on credential change, user notification on recovery actions, no-KBA, and GDPR/privacy alignment. Each item cites ASVS references.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section '## Open Gaps and Assumptions' contains 9 documented gaps/assumptions including: support-agent threat model not yet documented; MFA enrollment population unknown; lockout window inherited; email as sole channel limitation; agent identity-proofing signal list not yet drafted; audit retention period unspecified; concurrency/race conditions on token redemption; latency-budget interaction with breached-password screening; localization of notification emails for EU users.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 3, justified by payment volume / regulatory exposure / material impact
expectations.evidence: Section 1 'ASVS Level Decision' explicitly selects L3 as baseline. Rationale cites: ~$1.5B/year payout volume as a material-impact financial event; PCI-DSS in regulated zone; state money-transmitter regulations; SOC 2 audit pressure; bank-account-level blast radius (misdirected funds not clawed back); webhook attack surface. It also explicitly explains why L1 and L2 are insufficient (citing specific L3-only requirements like 4.1.5 per-message signing, 16.3.2 sensitive-data-access logging, 13.3.1/13.3.3 HSM-backed key management, 2.3.5 multi-user approval). All three justification axes (volume / regulatory / material impact) are clearly addressed.

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Feature-ASVS Coverage Matrix' is a proper markdown table with columns Feature | ASVS Section | Requirement | Level | Coverage | PRD Change Needed. ~30 rows including F-01 through F-05 plus 'All' rows for cross-cutting items. Each requirement is mapped to a specific ASVS chapter/section number and a Coverage status (Covered / Partial / Missing / N/A) plus the PRD change needed.

expectations.text: Output covers all five features F-01 through F-05 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Section 3 'Enhanced Feature Specifications' has all five features (F-01 List payouts, F-02 Get payout detail, F-03 Trigger on-demand payout, F-04 Update payout schedule, F-05 Webhook on payout state change). Each follows a consistent shape: Endpoint, Actor, Data touched, Trust boundaries crossed, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements (numbered), Acceptance Criteria (testable), Securability Notes (with SSEM attribute callouts). Shape is uniform across all five.

expectations.text: F-03 (trigger payout) requires idempotency keys to prevent duplicate payouts on retry
expectations.evidence: F-03 Updated Requirement #2 'Idempotency': 'idempotency_key MUST be required, validated as opaque string of bounded length, and de-duplicated for at least 24 hours. A replay of an identical request returns the original payout's response; a replay of a key with different body returns 409.' (2.3.1). The endpoint signature itself includes idempotency_key in the request body. Acceptance criterion explicitly tests: 'Replaying an identical signed request returns the same payout_id without creating a new payout (idempotency test).' Replay-with-different-body collision behavior (409) is also specified — a real-world correctness detail many PRDs miss.

expectations.text: F-03 enforces server-side derivation/verification of amount and destination (Derived Integrity) — client cannot impersonate or alter trust-critical state
expectations.evidence: F-03 Updated Requirement #4 mandates server-side BOLA on two objects: caller owns the source balance AND owns destination_bank_account_id AND that account is in 'verified' state. Requirement #5 'Originating-subject authorization' forbids god-mode service tokens for downstream calls. Requirement #7 enforces server-side per-merchant business-logic caps (daily/weekly/per-transaction) so the client cannot exceed limits by sending a higher amount. Requirement #9 enforces atomic balance debit / payout insert / ledger entry (integrity). Requirement #4 BOLA test is also asserted as an acceptance criterion. The Securability Notes explicitly call out Integrity (atomic updates and locking) and Authenticity (per-message signatures) as the dominant SSEM attributes for this flow.

expectations.text: F-05 (webhook) requires signed payloads with timing-safe signature verification and replay protection (timestamp / nonce)
expectations.evidence: F-05 Updated Requirement #4 mandates per-message signing with HMAC-SHA-256 (or Ed25519) over canonical timestamp + event_id + raw body, with key id in header for rotation. Requirement #5 mandates Timestamp header in signed string with 5-minute window, plus per-event event_id (>=128 bits CSPRNG) for receiver-side dedup. Acceptance criteria test tampered body fails verification and repeated event_id is treated as duplicate. HOWEVER: 'timing-safe' (constant-time) signature comparison is NOT explicitly required in the spec. The merchant-side verification guide (Requirement #10) is committed to but timing-safe compare guidance for receivers is not called out. This is the one gap against expectation #6.

expectations.text: API authentication strength is specified (e.g., scoped API keys, rotation, TLS-only) across endpoints
expectations.evidence: CC-02 'Authentication & authorization platform' specifies API keys are issued, stored hashed, scoped to a single merchant_id, scoped to a permission set (read, schedule-write, payout-initiate), and individually revocable. CC-01 mandates TLS 1.2+ only with no plaintext fallback and rejects rather than redirects HTTP for the API surface. CC-05 mandates HSM-backed vault storage and documented rotation schedules for all secrets including API-signing keys. F-03 escalates to per-message digital signatures plus step-up MFA over a threshold. F-04 forbids read-only keys from schedule-write. The per-feature ASVS Mapping references V6.1.1 documentation of auth strength.

expectations.text: Output flags rate limiting and anti-abuse controls on payout endpoints
expectations.evidence: F-01 Updated Requirement #7: 'Rate-limiting and anti-automation policy MUST be documented and enforced (e.g., per-key per-minute quota; lockout on signature/key failures with bounded backoff).' F-03 Updated Requirement #7 'Business-logic limits' enforces per-merchant daily/weekly/per-transaction caps server-side, with hits logged and surfaced as a distinct error code. F-04 Updated Requirement #7 mandates a scheduled-change rate limit (max N changes per merchant per day) to prevent griefing/oscillation. F-05 mandates circuit-break on repeated webhook delivery failures. Coverage matrix flags 6.1.1 anti-automation as Missing across F-01 and F-03.

expectations.text: Output flags comprehensive audit logging of payout state changes and admin actions
expectations.evidence: F-01 req 8/9 mandate structured access logs per request and sensitive-data-access audit (16.3.2 L3). F-02 req 6 requires one audit-log entry per detail view. F-03 req 13 requires every attempt (success and failure) log timestamp, merchant_id, key_id, idempotency_key, amount, masked destination, decision, signature-verification result, and outcome. F-04 req 5 requires audit log with before/after schedule values. F-05 req 9 logs every delivery attempt with target host, status, key_id, attempt, latency. CC-06 'Logging and audit' mandates logging inventory, append-only isolated log store, log injection encoding, and audit-grade events at L3. The Securability Notes for F-02 and F-03 explicitly call out Accountability (SSEM) and SOC 2 / PCI investigation needs.

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational
expectations.evidence: Each feature has concrete acceptance criteria with observables. Examples: F-01 'A payout owned by merchant B cannot be returned to merchant A under any combination of cursor, from, to (red-team test)'; 'Malformed from/to returns 400 with generic body; no stack trace; access log records the rejection.' F-02 'Probing for a known-good payout_id belonging to another merchant returns 404 with a body indistinguishable from a true not-found.' F-03 'Replaying an identical signed request returns the same payout_id without creating a new payout'; 'Two concurrent requests against the same balance result in exactly one debit (race test under load)'; 'A revoked key cannot initiate a payout 60 seconds after revocation.' F-04 'PUT with cadence: hourly returns 400 and a security-event log line.' F-05 'Registering http://10.0.0.5/x is rejected with 400; same for https://localhost, https://169.254.169.254 (cloud metadata), and https://attacker@evil.tld'; DNS-rebinding test harness; circuit-break after 5 consecutive 5xx triggers internal alert.

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section covering TLS, secrets, and observability
expectations.evidence: Section 4 'Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' has 11 numbered subsections: CC-01 Transport security (TLS 1.2+, no plaintext fallback, internal CA pinning, no auto-redirect for API endpoints); CC-02 AuthN/AuthZ platform; CC-03 Input validation; CC-04 Cryptographic agility; CC-05 Secret management (HSM-backed vault, no source/image baking, scanning, rotation schedules); CC-06 Logging and audit (inventory, structured fields, redaction, append-only isolated store, log injection encoding, audit-grade events); CC-07 Error handling; CC-08 Data classification; CC-09 Configuration hardening; CC-10 Multi-tenant isolation; CC-11 Securability quality gates (FIASSE/SSEM). All three required topics — TLS (CC-01), secrets (CC-05), observability/logging (CC-06) — are covered in dedicated subsections with specific ASVS citations.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: Section 5 'Open Gaps and Assumptions' has both halves. 'Open gaps' (7 items): high-value approval threshold, idempotency window, webhook retry budget, signature algorithm choice, logging retention, authorization cache TTL, out-of-scope dependencies (bank-verification PRD, onboarding flow). 'Assumptions' (4 items): PCI-tokenization vendor scope, API key + signed request only (not OAuth2), logs ship to isolated SIEM, webhook signing keys are merchant-specific. Plus 'Ratified non-functional requirements (from original PRD, retained)' explicitly preserves the original p99 latency, 99.95% availability, and 60s webhook delivery targets.

expectations.text: Chosen ASVS level is 3, justified by payment volume / regulatory exposure / material impact
expectations.evidence: Section 'ASVS Level Decision' states 'Chosen Level: 3' with rationale explicitly tying it to ~$120M/month payout volume, PCI-DSS scope, money-transmitter regulations, severe material impact (financial loss, reportable regulatory incidents, SOC 2 findings), and L3 threat-actor profile. Also explicitly rules out L1 and L2 with reasoning.

expectations.text: Output contains a feature-to-ASVS coverage matrix as a markdown table
expectations.evidence: Section 'Feature x ASVS Coverage Matrix' contains a markdown table with columns Feature, ASVS Section, Requirement ID, Level, Coverage, PRD Change Needed, populated for F-01 through F-05 with ~58 rows.

expectations.text: Output covers all five features F-01 through F-05 with the prescribed per-feature shape
expectations.evidence: Each of F-01 through F-05 has the same five-section shape: Actor / Data / Trust Boundaries header block, ASVS Mapping, Updated Requirements (R-XX.N IDs), Acceptance Criteria, Securability Notes. Consistent IDs (R-01.x, R-02.x, R-03.x, R-04.x, R-05.x) and structure across all five.

expectations.text: F-03 (trigger payout) requires idempotency keys to prevent duplicate payouts on retry
expectations.evidence: R-03.4 (V11.5.1, idempotency): 'Every request must include an Idempotency-Key header (UUIDv4 or 128-bit CSPRNG token). The server stores the key with the resulting payout ID for at least 24 hours; a duplicate key with an identical payload returns the original payout ID; a duplicate key with a different payload returns 409 Conflict and is logged as an anomaly.' Acceptance criteria include concurrent-same-key and conflicting-payload-same-key tests.

expectations.text: F-03 enforces server-side derivation/verification of amount and destination (Derived Integrity) - client cannot impersonate or alter trust-critical state
expectations.evidence: R-03.2 specifies server-side schema validation of amount, currency, destination_bank_account_id including positive integer minor units, per-merchant cap, ISO 4217 allowlist, server-issued opaque shape, currency-destination consistency. R-03.3 enforces server-side authorization that the merchant owns both balance and destination, re-checked on every request and 'never trusted from client-supplied state'. Securability Notes explicitly call out FIASSE Derived Integrity (S6.4.1.1) and 'amount, destination_bank_account_id, and (worst of all) available balance must never be trusted from client-supplied or client-cached state'.

expectations.text: F-05 (webhook) requires signed payloads with timing-safe signature verification and replay protection (timestamp / nonce)
expectations.evidence: R-05.2 mandates HMAC-SHA-256 over canonical payload + timestamp, allowlist of algorithms, alg:none and weak hashes forbidden. R-05.3 includes timestamp + audience binding with documented +/-5-minute tolerance window for replay protection. Acceptance criterion: 'replaying the same payload after a 6-minute delay is rejected'. However, the requirement that signature comparison itself be performed in constant time (timing-safe / constant-time comparison) is NOT stated. Nonce is also not explicitly named as a separate replay control - only timestamp tolerance is.

expectations.text: API authentication strength is specified (e.g., scoped API keys, rotation, TLS-only) across endpoints
expectations.evidence: CC-01 (V13.2.1, V8.3.1): 'Replace static API key auth with short-lived bearer tokens (e.g., OAuth2 client credentials with rotating client secrets, or signed-request scheme with per-request nonce). API keys, if retained for backward compat, are scoped per merchant, rotatable, and revocable in real time.' CC-02 covers identity propagation. CC-11 covers TLS 1.2+/1.3 inbound and outbound. CC-09/CC-10 cover secrets storage in HSM-backed vault and rotation schedules.

expectations.text: Output flags rate limiting and anti-abuse controls on payout endpoints
expectations.evidence: R-01.6 per-credential and per-IP rate limit on F-01 with concrete numbers (60 req/min). R-03.6 documented per-merchant velocity limits on F-03 (max N/24h, max cumulative $/24h, 429 with error_code=velocity_limit_exceeded). R-03.8 anti-automation control rejecting <1s consecutive POSTs. R-04.6 cadence-change rate limit (5 per 30 days). CC-12 cross-cutting: every endpoint has a documented per-credential and per-IP rate limit with 429 + Retry-After.

expectations.text: Output flags comprehensive audit logging of payout state changes and admin actions
expectations.evidence: R-01.13 logs every list call with structured metadata. R-02.6 logs every detail fetch including denials. R-03.10 logs every accepted/rejected/rate-limited/validation-failed/ownership-denied/idempotency-conflict/step-up-required event with comprehensive metadata. R-04.8 logs schedule changes with prior+new value and out-of-band notification. R-05.9 logs every webhook send. CC-04 (logging inventory), CC-05 (structured format), CC-06 (no sensitive data), CC-07 (auth/authz/rate-limit/step-up outcomes), CC-08 (write-once tamper-evident, separated infrastructure).

expectations.text: Acceptance criteria are testable (specific outcomes/observables) rather than aspirational
expectations.evidence: Sample acceptance criteria: 'Submitting amount=-100 or amount=0 returns 400 with error_code=invalid_amount; no balance debit; no payout record; one payout_create_rejected audit log line.' '100 concurrent requests against a merchant with $X balance and $X payout amount each result in exactly one successful payout; the rest receive 409 Conflict or 422 insufficient_balance; total debited is exactly $X.' 'curl --tlsv1.1 fails to negotiate.' 'Configuring a webhook URL of http://..., https://localhost/..., https://10.x.x.x/..., or https://169.254.169.254/... is rejected with error_code=webhook_url_disallowed.'

expectations.text: Output includes a cross-cutting securability requirements section covering TLS, secrets, and observability
expectations.evidence: 'Cross-Cutting Securability Requirements' section explicitly contains: 'TLS and Transport (V12)' subsection with CC-11, 'Secrets and Key Management (V13.3, V11)' subsection with CC-09 and CC-10, 'Logging and Audit (V16.1, V16.2, V16.3, V16.4)' subsection with CC-04 through CC-08. Also includes Authentication and Identity, Step-Up, Rate Limiting, Error Handling, Configuration / Information Disclosure, Data Classification, and Dependency / Supply Chain.

expectations.text: Output includes an open gaps / assumptions section
expectations.evidence: 'Open Gaps and Assumptions' section enumerates 13 numbered items: authentication mechanism details, step-up factor selection, high-value threshold for F-03, velocity limits numbers, idempotency window length, webhook signature scheme details, PCI-DSS scoping confirmation, state money-transmitter regulations, out-of-band notification channel, webhook URL change flow not in PRD, merchant role model not surfaced, bank-account change flow dependency, test environment and test-data policy.
